---
title: "Start & Ready Commands"
description: "Control long-running processes and snapshot readiness when building Go templates."
---

# Start & Ready Commands

In the Go SDK, template start and ready commands are configured on `*e2b.TemplateBase`:

- `SetStartCmd(startCommand, readyCommand...)`
- `SetReadyCmd(readyCommand)`

These commands belong to the template build, not the normal sandbox runtime API.

## Start command

The start command is captured as part of the template build flow. It is intended for long-running processes that should already be present in the template snapshot, such as:

- web servers
- seeded databases
- background daemons

If you need a command to run each time you create or connect to a sandbox, use `sandbox.Commands.Run(...)` after the sandbox exists instead of `SetStartCmd(...)`.

```go
package main

import (
	"context"

	e2b "github.com/superduck-ai/e2b-go-sdk"
)

func main() {
	ctx := context.Background()

	sandbox, err := e2b.Connect(ctx, "sbx_123", nil)
	if err != nil {
		return
	}

	_, _ = sandbox.Commands.Run(ctx, "printenv APP_ENV", nil)
}
```

If the process needs environment variables during template build, set them on the template with `SetEnvs(...)`.

## Ready command

The ready command controls when the build can continue to snapshotting. In practice, use it to wait for a process, port, file, URL, or fixed delay before the template is finalized.

In Go, the ready command can be:

- a raw `string`
- `*e2b.ReadyCmd` from one of the `WaitFor*` helpers

## Use `SetStartCmd(...)`

Use `SetStartCmd(...)` when you want to define both:

- the long-running process started during template build
- the condition that marks it ready

```go
package main

import (
	e2b "github.com/superduck-ai/e2b-go-sdk"
)

func main() {
	template := e2b.Template(nil).
		FromUbuntuImage("22.04").
		SetEnvs(map[string]string{
			"APP_ENV": "production",
		}).
		AptInstall([]string{"curl", "python3"}, nil).
		SetStartCmd("python3 -m http.server 8000", e2b.WaitForPort(8000))

	_ = template
}
```

You can also supply a custom shell command instead of a helper:

```go
package main

import (
	e2b "github.com/superduck-ai/e2b-go-sdk"
)

func main() {
	template := e2b.Template(nil).
		FromNodeImage().
		SetStartCmd("npm start", "curl -fsS http://localhost:3000/health")

	_ = template
}
```

Common patterns:

```go
package main

import (
	e2b "github.com/superduck-ai/e2b-go-sdk"
)

func main() {
	template := e2b.Template(nil)

	template.SetStartCmd("npx next --turbo", e2b.WaitForURL("http://localhost:3000"))
	template.SetStartCmd("python -m http.server 8000", e2b.WaitForPort(8000))
	template.SetStartCmd("/start_command.sh", e2b.WaitForPort(6080))
}
```

## Use `SetReadyCmd(...)`

Use `SetReadyCmd(...)` when you do not need to define a start command, but you still want to delay snapshotting until the system reaches a desired state.

This is useful when earlier build steps already start a background process.

```go
package main

import (
	e2b "github.com/superduck-ai/e2b-go-sdk"
)

func main() {
	template := e2b.Template(nil).
		FromUbuntuImage("22.04").
		RunCmd("apt-get install -y nginx && service nginx start", &struct{ User string }{User: "root"}).
		SetReadyCmd(e2b.WaitForPort(80))

	_ = template
}
```

More examples:

```go
package main

import (
	e2b "github.com/superduck-ai/e2b-go-sdk"
)

func main() {
	template := e2b.Template(nil)

	template.SetReadyCmd(e2b.WaitForFile("/tmp/ready"))
	template.SetReadyCmd(e2b.WaitForTimeout(10_000))
	template.SetReadyCmd("curl -fsS http://localhost:8080/health")
}
```

## Ready command helpers

The helper functions can be used with both `SetStartCmd(...)` and `SetReadyCmd(...)`:

```go
package main

import (
	e2b "github.com/superduck-ai/e2b-go-sdk"
)

func main() {
	_ = e2b.WaitForPort(3000)
	_ = e2b.WaitForURL("http://localhost:3000/health")
	_ = e2b.WaitForURL("http://localhost:3000/health", 200)
	_ = e2b.WaitForProcess("node")
	_ = e2b.WaitForFile("/tmp/ready")
	_ = e2b.WaitForTimeout(10_000)
}
```

Important Go details:

- `WaitForURL(...)` defaults to status `200`
- `WaitForTimeout(...)` accepts milliseconds
- `WaitForTimeout(500)` becomes a one-second wait because the current Go SDK floors to whole seconds and enforces a minimum of one second

For the exact helper surface, see the [Go SDK Template ReadyCmd reference](/docs/sdk-reference/go-sdk/template-readycmd).
